topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The studio air felt suddenly thin, the familiar hum of the equipment amplifying the silence where my melody used to live. I was tweaking a final vocal take, a fragile piece of emotion I’d poured into the track, when it happened. Through the closed door, I heard it—my song. Not a cover, not a tribute, but my exact arrangement, the subtle chord shifts, the way I’d layered the reverb on the chorus. A live band, raw and powerful, was playing it perfectly. My hands froze over the mixing board, the stylus hovering uselessly above the transport controls. A cold trickle of sweat traced a path down my spine. I pushed back from the console, my breath catching in my throat, and stared blankly at the wall, the music still echoing in the cavern of my ears, alien and utterly mine.

[story 2]

I was nursing lukewarm coffee, trying to force myself to focus on lyric revisions, when the sound bled through the thin insulation of my apartment walls. It was my track, the one I’d spent six months agonizing over, being spun out by an unknown ensemble down the street. The initial recognition was a dizzying rush, followed by a profound disorientation. I dropped my pen. It clattered loudly on the wooden floor. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm entirely separate from the bass line vibrating through the floorboards. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, my gaze darting to the window, searching for the source of the intrusion, the reality of it all feeling suddenly porous and unstable.

[story 3]

The headphones were on, volume low, just testing a new bridge section. Then, a sudden, jarring intrusion. A full band, playing my composition, somewhere in the park across the street. The sound hit me like a physical blow. I ripped the headphones off, my fingers trembling as they fumbled with the cord. For a long moment, I just stood there, rooted to the spot, listening to the familiar notes being interpreted by unfamiliar voices. A tightness constricted my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I walked to the window, peering out into the mundane street scene, trying to reconcile the flawless execution of my work with the fact that someone else was breathing it into the air, making it real somewhere else.
